title: Practical Rust and Design
description: Questions about real-world Rust development, project structure, ecosystem, and design decisions.

questions.question: How do you structure a large Rust project?
questions.answer:
A well-structured Rust project uses Cargo workspaces and clear module boundaries:

Workspace (for multi-crate projects):
my-project/
  Cargo.toml          (workspace definition)
  crates/
    core/             (domain logic, no I/O)
    api/              (HTTP handlers)
    db/               (database layer)
    cli/              (binary entry point)

Single crate structure:
src/
  main.rs             (thin entry point: parse args, call lib)
  lib.rs              (public API, re-exports)
  config.rs           (configuration)
  error.rs            (error types)
  models/
    mod.rs
    user.rs
  services/
    mod.rs
    user_service.rs
  handlers/
    mod.rs

Principles:
- main.rs is a thin wrapper: parse args, set up logging, call into lib
- Put logic in lib.rs so it is testable without running the binary
- Group by domain (user, order) or by layer (model, service, handler)
- Use pub(crate) for internal APIs that should not be exposed

questions.question: Compare Rust's enums with enums in other languages (Java, TypeScript). What makes Rust enums special?
questions.answer:
Rust enums are algebraic data types (sum types) -- each variant can hold different data:

enum Shape {
    Circle { radius: f64 },
    Rectangle { width: f64, height: f64 },
    Triangle(f64, f64, f64),
    Point,
}

Compared to Java/TypeScript enums:

1. Data carrying: Rust enum variants hold data. Java enums are essentially fixed constants. TypeScript enums are just named numbers/strings.

2. Exhaustive matching: match on a Rust enum must handle all variants (the compiler enforces this). Adding a variant breaks all incomplete matches, catching bugs at compile time.

3. No null: Option<T> replaces null. Result<T, E> replaces exceptions. Both are enums.

4. Pattern matching: deeply nested destructuring with guards:
   match shape {
       Shape::Circle { radius } if radius > 10.0 => ...,
       Shape::Rectangle { width, height } => ...,
       _ => ...,
   }

5. Zero overhead: enum size equals the largest variant + a discriminant tag. No heap allocation.

questions.question: What is the difference between a library crate and a binary crate?
questions.answer:
Binary crate (src/main.rs): compiles to an executable. Has a fn main() entry point. This is what users run.

Library crate (src/lib.rs): compiles to a library that other crates depend on. No main() function. Exposes a public API via pub items.

A project can have both: src/main.rs uses the library from src/lib.rs. This is the recommended pattern because:
- The library is independently testable
- Other projects can depend on your library
- Integration tests (tests/) can access the library
- main.rs stays thin (arg parsing + calling library)

A workspace can contain multiple binary crates and library crates. Each is a separate compilation unit with its own Cargo.toml.

questions.question: How would you handle configuration in a Rust application?
When would you choose Rust over Go, Python, or C++?
questions.answer:
Choose Rust over Python when:
- You need high performance (10-100x faster)
- You need memory safety without GC pauses
- You are building systems software, CLI tools, or WebAssembly

Choose Rust over Go when:
- You need zero-cost abstractions and no GC
- You need fine-grained memory control
- The type system expressiveness matters (enums, traits, generics)
- You are building embedded systems, OS components, or game engines

Choose Rust over C++ when:
- Memory safety is critical (no use-after-free, no buffer overflows)
- You want modern tooling (cargo, crates.io, clippy, rustfmt)
- You want thread safety guaranteed at compile time
- Starting a new project (C++ has huge legacy codebases to consider)

Choose something else when:
- Rapid prototyping is the priority (Python)
- Your team does not know Rust and time-to-market is critical
- The ecosystem you need is stronger elsewhere (ML = Python, enterprise = Java)
- Compile times are a major concern (Go compiles faster)

questions.question: What is unsafe Rust and when is it acceptable to use?
questions.answer:
unsafe Rust unlocks five capabilities the compiler cannot verify: raw pointer dereferencing, calling unsafe functions, mutable static access, unsafe trait implementation, and union field access.

Acceptable uses:
1. FFI: calling C libraries (the compiler cannot verify foreign code)
2. Performance-critical code where bounds checking is the bottleneck AND you can prove safety
3. Implementing low-level abstractions (custom allocators, lock-free data structures)
4. Working with hardware (memory-mapped I/O, device registers)

Not acceptable:
- Bypassing the borrow checker because it is 'annoying' (redesign your data structures instead)
- Performance optimization without profiling first
- When a safe alternative exists with acceptable performance

Best practices:
- Keep unsafe blocks minimal and well-documented
- Wrap unsafe code in safe public APIs
- Document all safety invariants with // SAFETY: comments
- Test with Miri to detect undefined behavior
- Get code review from experienced Rust developers

questions.question: Describe Rust's module system and visibility rules.
questions.answer:
Modules organize code into namespaces. They form a tree rooted at lib.rs or main.rs.

Declaration:
- mod foo; in parent looks for src/foo.rs or src/foo/mod.rs
- mod foo { ... } defines inline module

Visibility:
- Private by default (only accessible within the same module and its children)
- pub: visible to everyone
- pub(crate): visible within the crate only
- pub(super): visible to the parent module
- pub(in path): visible within a specific path

Re-exports:
- pub use crate::internal::Thing; exposes Thing at the crate root
- Lets you restructure internals without breaking the public API

Key rules:
- Child modules can access private items in parent modules
- Parent modules CANNOT access private items in child modules
- Struct fields are private by default (even on pub structs)
- Enum variants inherit the enum's visibility (if pub enum, all variants are pub)

The prelude pattern: pub mod prelude { pub use crate::ImportantTrait; pub use crate::CommonType; } lets users write use my_crate::prelude::*;
